entry.title: SAP DM - get sequence of re-assembled BOM component
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

Below is the scenario:

Scenario: 

step-1: Main line assembled the part on the production, but later on issue found out and part is sent to Repair station.

step-2: At repair station, assembled part was removed and new part was assembled (reassembly).

Repair station is only sending  component Name, assembly data and Qty to SAP DM - 

Questions are - 

  1. how to identify the sequence of the BOM component in SAP DM as we have same component is getting assembled on the operation. 
  2. is there any direct API, that gives us the how many components are assembled and how many are remaining with sequenced ID defined in BOM. 

Please let us know is there any way to get the sequence of BOM Components defined in Components tab of SFC using API/or SAP DM MDO's

Quick response will be appreciated. 

Regards,
Ankit Gupta

entry.title: Debugging Script Tool – Uses and Overview
entry.content:
The SAP ABAP Debugger Scripting Tool is a powerful and advanced feature available in the New ABAP Debugger (introduced from SAP NetWeaver 7.0 onwards). It enables developers to write and execute ABAP code directly within the debugging session. 

Unlike traditional debugging, where developers manually step through the program using F5 (Single Step) or F6 (Execute), the Debugger Scripting Tool allows automation of repetitive or complex debugging tasks. 

It can be considered as a “Debugger for the Debugger”, because it enhances debugging capabilities by enabling: 

  • Automated variable monitoring 
  • Conditional break logic 
  • Mass data validation 
  • Dynamic analysis of internal tables 
  • Runtime data manipulation for analysis 

This significantly improves efficiency when analyzing complex programs, large datasets, or issues that occur inside loops. 

Key Benefits 

  • Automation of Repetitive Checks – No need to manually inspect variables repeatedly inside loops. 
  • Faster Root Cause Analysis – Quickly identify inconsistencies in large internal tables. 
  • Custom Logic Execution During Debugging – Execute custom ABAP logic without modifying the original program. 
  • Time Saving – Reduces manual effort in complex debugging scenarios. 

Inbuilt Scripts (Standard Script Samples) 

SAP provides several Standard Script Samples that are built directly into the debugger. These are pre-delivered template scripts designed to handle common debugging scenarios. 

 Business Scenario: 

How to Stop Execution at a Particular DB Operation (SELECT / INSERT / UPDATE / DELETE) 

In ABAP debugging, sometimes we need to stop execution exactly when a specific database operation is executed — such as a SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE statement. 

This is especially useful when: 

  • Data is not getting updated correctly 
  • Unexpected records are being fetched 
  • Duplicate entries are inserted 
  • Incorrect deletions are happening 

 Example by considering MM03 Transaction Code 
 

Go to Transaction Code MM03. 

Enter the Material Number in the required field. 

Type /h in the command field to activate the debugger. 

 

Click on Enter The system will trigger the debugger.  

Go to the Script tab in the Debugger 

Click on Load Script.  

Click on Overview button  

Expand  Generic breakpoints.  

Select “RSTPDA_SCRIPT_BP_SELECT_TAB”   

Click on Start Script 

Specify database table name where the debugger should stop when a SELECT query runs.  

 

Press the “Continue Script” button every time to continue execution. 
Note: If you press F8, the debugger will stop at each SELECT query. 

By following the same steps, we can try this for other database tables as well.  

 

Similarly, we can use the same approach for other database operations such as INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE. 

 By specifying the required database table and operation type in the script, the debugger will stop whenever that particular INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE statement is executed during program execution.  

Conclusion:

The Debugging Script Tool in SAP is very useful for analyzing database operations during program execution. By specifying the required database table and operation (SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE), developers can easily stop the debugger exactly where the database interaction happens. This helps in understanding program flow, identifying issues, and debugging efficiently without manually searching through large amounts of code.

entry.title: Bulk Send Documents to Multiple Signers using Docusign Adapter
entry.content:
The Docusign Adapter for SAP Integration Suite, simplifies and accelerates integration while ensuring secure authentication and connectivity with DocuSign. It enables seamless embedding of e-signature capabilities into your workflows. The adapter streamlines processes such as contract signing, purchase order approvals, and other document-based transactions, resulting in faster processing, enhanced compliance, and a smoother user experience.

Bulk Send using Docusign Adapter

In this scenario, we will demonstrate how to create a template (containing a signable pdf), and send it to multiple signers. We will add the signer's email to the bulk send list used for the bulk send request.

Creating Credentials to connect to Docusign

We will use OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code for this scenario.

OAuth Authentication: The OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code flow is a secure method for applications, especially server-based ones, to access a user’s data without directly handling their credentials.  For more info, see section 3.3 in the Adapter Guide.

To create the Oauth2 Authorization Code in your SAP environment, specify the artifact Name, Authorization URL, Token Service URL, and the Client ID (Integration Key) from your DocuSign account. Refer figure below to configure the settings.

Iflow Design in SAP CI 

The figure below demonstrates provides a representation of how the adapter works.

Let us break down the three parts to this scenario. 

  • We will create a template in Docusign which has a pdf (encoded as base64); the template must have the signer roles added to it. We will add three roles since we want it to be sent to 3 approvers. You can modify this part to add more.
  • Secondly, we create a bulk send list , which contains the Names and emails of the recepients. Note that the number of signers in the template must match those in the bulk send list.
  • Thirdly, we  use the templateID for step#1 and listId from step#2 to send a bulk request for signature.

Connect to Docusign via Docusign Adapter

Now you must configure the Connection tab of the adapter, specify Address and select Authentication as OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code. Specify the Authorization Code Credentials that was created previously in SAP Security Material. For more information about Connection tab parameters, see Connection in SAP Help.

Convert your PDF to Base64

Create a PDF and convert it into base64; the signer tab position can be set in the payload.

Operation 1:  Create a Template in DocuSign (with a PDF)

In the Content Modifier, you can define the name, description and signer fields.

{
  "name": "Student Activities Form",
  "description": "Form for bulk signing with anchor-based signature fields",
  "emailSubject": "Student Activities Form",
  "emailBlurb": "Dear Student, please enter the details and sign",
  "status": "created",
  "documents": [
    {
      "documentId": "1",
      "name": "Student_Form.pdf",
      "fileExtension": "pdf",
      "documentBase64": "<Your base 64 encoded content goes here>"
    }
  ],
  "recipients": {
    "signers": [
      {
        "roleName": "Signer 1",
        "recipientId": "1",
        "routingOrder": "1",
        "tabs": {
          "signHereTabs": [
            {
              "documentId": "1",
              "tabLabel": "SignatureField",
              "anchorString": "/SignHere/",
              "anchorXOffset": "4.3",
              "anchorYOffset": "10.5",
              "anchorUnits": "cms",
              "anchorIgnoreIfNotPresent": "false"
            }
          ],
          "dateSignedTabs": [
            {
              "documentId": "1",
              "tabLabel": "DateSigned",
              "anchorString": "/DateSigned/",
              "anchorXOffset": "4.3",
              "anchorYOffset": "11.5",
              "anchorUnits": "cms",
              "anchorIgnoreIfNotPresent": "false"
            }
          ],
          "fullNameTabs": [
            {
              "documentId": "1",
              "tabLabel": "FullName",
              "anchorString": "/FullName/",
              "anchorXOffset": "4.3",
              "anchorYOffset": "12.5",
              "anchorUnits": "cms",
              "anchorIgnoreIfNotPresent": "false"
            }
          ],
          "titleTabs": [
            {
              "documentId": "1",
              "tabLabel": "Title",
              "anchorString": "/Title/",
              "anchorXOffset": "4.3",
              "anchorYOffset": "13.5",
              "anchorUnits": "cms",
              "anchorIgnoreIfNotPresent": "false"
            }
          ],
          "textTabs": [
            {
              "documentId": "1",
              "tabLabel": "Name",
              "anchorString": "/Name/",
              "anchorXOffset": "4.3",
              "anchorYOffset": "14.5",
              "anchorUnits": "cms",
              "anchorIgnoreIfNotPresent": "false",
              "required": "false",
              "locked": "false",
              "width": "150",
              "height": "20"
            },
            {
              "documentId": "1",
              "tabLabel": "Value",
              "anchorString": "/Value/",
              "anchorXOffset": "5.3",
              "anchorYOffset": "15.5",
              "anchorUnits": "cms",
              "anchorIgnoreIfNotPresent": "false",
              "required": "false",
              "locked": "false",
              "width": "150",
              "height": "20"
            }
          ]
        }
      }
    ]
  },
  "notification": {
    "useAccountDefaults": "false",
    "reminders": {
      "reminderEnabled": "true",
      "reminderDelay": "2",
      "reminderFrequency": "3"
    },
    "expirations": {
      "expireEnabled": "true",
      "expireAfter": "30",
      "expireWarn": "5"
    }
  }
}

Response: Once the template is created, the response will contain the TemplateId. Store this TemplateId in a header for use in subsequent steps.

Navigate to the next request reply pallet and in processing, select the "Create one or more templates" operation.

Operation 2: Create a Bulk Send List with Recipient Emails

Now, let create a list of receipts to create a bulk send list. In the Content Modifier, you can define and configure the recipient details, such as names, email addresses, or identifiers, to ensure the document is sent to the intended parties accurately and efficiently.

{
  "name": "Student Bulk List - Activities",
  "bulkCopies": [
    {
      "recipients": [
        {
          "roleName": "Signer 1",
          "name": "Signer1",
          "email": "Singer1@example.com"
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "recipients": [
        {
          "roleName": "Signer 1",
          "name": "Signer2",
          "email": "Singer2@example.com"
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "recipients": [
        {
          "roleName": "Signer 1",
          "name": "Signer3",
          "email": "Singer3@example.com"
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}

Response: Once the Send List is created, the response will contain the BulkSendListId. Store this ListId in a header for use in subsequent steps.

Navigate to the next request reply pallet and in processing, select the "Create bulk send list" operation.

Operation 3:  Send Template to Multiple Signers/Recipients

Now let us send our document to multiple users.

In a Content Modifier, you need to use the TemplateId that was previously stored in the header.

{
  "envelopeOrTemplateId": "${header.templateID}",
  "mailingListId": "${header.ListId}",
  "senderDisplaySettings": {
    "senderName": "<SenderName>",
    "senderEmailAddress": "<SenderEmail>"
  }
}

Navigate to the next request reply pallet and in processing, select the "Create bulk send request" operation.

Response: Once the Send Request is created, we will receive the requestId to track further.

Result

Once the above process is completed, the signers will receive an email to sign the document.

Click Continue.

Once signed, click Finish. 

Final Thoughts

This Iflow can be modified for scenarios where you want to send documents for a larger list of recipients. For more information, see the Docusign Adapter for SAP Integration Suite.

entry.title: RAP New Feature : Generating Managed RAP Applications With Table Entity
entry.content:
Traditionally, when developing a managed RAP application, we create multiple artifacts such as CDS view entities, behavior definitions, service definitions, and service bindings. However, with the introduction of Table Entity, SAP allows us to directly expose database tables in a RAP-compliant way, significantly reducing boilerplate development effort.

This feature is especially useful when:

  • You want to quickly generate a managed RAP application

  • The table structure already satisfies your business requirements

  • You want to minimize intermediate CDS modeling layers

  • You are building internal or utility applications with simple transactional needs

    In this blog, we will explore:

    • What a Table Entity is in RAP

    • How it differs from traditional CDS view entities

    • Step-by-step creation of a Managed RAP application using Table Entity

    • Service exposure and preview in Fiori Elements

      Table Entity:

      • Read and write access is possible using ABAP SQL SELECT, UPDATE, MODIFY, INSERT, and DELETE.

      • A table entity can be used as a data type in ABAP.

        Properties of Table Entities:

        Key Fields – You can define one or more elements as the primary key using the keyword KEY. However, unlike database tables, key fields are optional and it is also possible to have a table entity without a primary key.

        Note: The access pattern of a table entity without a primary key is that it is always read completely, which can result in longer processing times. Single record access is not provided.

        Client Dependency

        Client handling is defined using the header annotation @ClientHandling.type. The possible annotation values are #CLIENT_DEPENDENT or #CLIENT_INDEPENDENT. 

        Flag for Null Values

        A null value on the database is an undefined value. Columns of table entities are by default created as NOT NULL on the SAP HANA database. It is possible to override this default behavior by using the optional addition NULL for a column. 

        Delivery Class

        The delivery class of a table entity controls the transport of table data in installations, upgrades, or client copies, and in transports between customer systems. Annotation  @AbapCatalog.deliveryClass is used to specify delivery class.

         

        • select table entity template.
@ClientHandling.type: #CLIENT_DEPENDENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass: #APPLICATION_DATA
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'travel information'
define table entity ztb_travel
{
  key travel_id : /dmo/travel_id;
  description   : /dmo/description;
  status        : /dmo/travel_status;
    lastchangedat : timestampl;
 

​

 

  • create report/class to insert entries.

 

select travel_id, description, status,lastchangedat FROM /dmo/travel into TABLE (lt_travel).
if sy-subrc eq 0.
insert ztb_travel FROM TABLE _TRAVEL.
if sy-subrc eq 0.
out->write( 'travel data inserted successfully' ).
else.
out->write( 'failed to insert' ).
ENDIF.
endif.



  • Lets make our table entity as root entity so that will create rap application as well

     
@ClientHandling.type: #CLIENT_DEPENDENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass: #APPLICATION_DATA
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'travel information'
define root table entity ztb_travel
{
  key travel_id : /dmo/travel_id;
  description   : /dmo/description;
  status        : /dmo/travel_status;
    lastchangedat : timestampl;    
}

 

Projection View:

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'consumption view for travel table entity'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define root view entity zcb_travel as projection on ztb_travel
{

@UI.facet: [{ label: 'Travel Info', type: #IDENTIFICATION_REFERENCE }]
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 10 }]
@UI.identification: [{ position: 10 }]
    key travel_id,
    .lineItem: [{ position: 20 }]
@UI.identification: [{ position: 20 }]
    
    description,
    .lineItem: [{ position: 30 }]
@UI.identification: [{ position: 30 }]
    
    status
}

 

Interface Behavior Definition:

managed implementation in class zbp_tb_travel unique;
strict ( 2 );
with draft;
define behavior for ztb_travel alias tb_travel
lock master total etag lastchangedat
draft table zdtbtrav
authorization master ( instance )
{
  create ( authorization : global );
  update;
  delete;
  field ( readonly ) lastchangedat;
  draft action Activate optimized;
  draft action Discard;
  draft action Edit;
  draft action Resume;
  draft determine action Prepare;
}


Projection Behavior Definition:

projection implementation in class zbp_cb_travel unique;
strict ( 2 );
use draft;

define behavior for zcb_travel alias tb_travel
{
  use create;
  use update;
  use delete;

  use action Activate;
  use action Discard;
  use action Edit;
  use action Resume;
  use action Prepare;
}


Service Definition:

 

@EndUserText.label: 'sd for table entity travel'
define service Zsd_tb_trav {
  expose zcb_travel as TableEntityTravel;
}

 
Service Binding:

 

generate service binding and test,we generate managed rap application, CRUD operations will be handled by framework itself.

Conclusion:
the table entity in RAP provides a simple and effective way to expose database tables directly in RAP-based applications. It helps developers quickly build transactional services with minimal configuration while still benefiting from the RAP framework’s capabilities. By using table entities, developers can accelerate development and maintain consistency within modern SAP application architecture.
ABAP Connectivity  ABAP Extensibility  SAP BTP ABAP environment ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model

entry.title: Re: Impact of Material Type in MM01 on SAP Configuration...
entry.content:
1) Key technical objects influenced by Material Type

Configuration Table: T134 (Material Types) – defines attributes of each material type.
Number Range Assignment: T134G links material type to number ranges.
Field Selection: T130F controls which fields are mandatory/optional/suppressed in MM01/MM02.
Allowed Views: Material type determines which master data views (e.g., Accounting, Purchasing, Sales) can be maintained.
Procurement Type Defaults: Material type can predefine procurement type (in-house vs. external).
Valuation Class Link: Material type + valuation area determines valuation class, which drives account determination.

2) Impact on Valuation, Accounting, and Stock Management

Valuation: Material type decides whether a material is valuated (e.g., raw materials vs. non-valuated consumables).
Accounting: Through valuation class, material type influences which G/L accounts are hit during goods movements.
Stock Management: Material type controls whether quantity/value updating occurs in inventory management. For example, non-stock materials (NLAG) don’t update inventory quantities.

3) Relationship with Account Determination (OBYC)

Automatic Account Determination: OBYC uses valuation class (linked to material type) to determine G/L accounts for transactions like GR, GI, and invoice posting.
Example: Raw materials (ROH) → valuation class 3000 → stock account 310000. Finished goods (FERT) → valuation class 7920 → finished goods account 790000.
Material type indirectly drives OBYC by restricting which valuation classes can be assigned.

4) Influence on Procurement Processes

Purchasing: Material type defines if a material can be procured externally (e.g., ROH) or only produced internally (e.g., HALB).
Production: For semifinished/finished goods, material type ensures integration with PP (production orders, BOMs).
Consumables: Material type like NLAG (non-stock) ensures direct consumption posting without inventory update.

5) Influence on Sales Processes

Sales Views Availability
Material type controls whether the Sales: Sales Org Data and Sales: General/Plant Data views are available in the material master. Without these views, you cannot maintain sales‑related data (pricing, sales unit, delivering plant, etc.).

Sales Document Item Categories
Certain material types are linked to specific item category determination in SD. For example:

Finished goods (FERT) → standard item category (TAN).
Non‑stock materials (NLAG) → item category that posts directly to consumption, not inventory.
This influences how sales orders behave and how billing documents are created.

Pricing and Account Determination
Material type indirectly influences pricing because it determines valuation class and stock behavior. In SD billing, the valuation class flows into FI postings (via OBYC), ensuring the correct revenue and inventory accounts are hit.

Availability Check / ATP
Material type defines whether stock is managed and updated. For example, non‑stock materials (NLAG) won’t trigger ATP checks in sales orders, while finished goods (FERT) will.

Integration with Logistics Execution
Material type affects whether delivery processing is possible. For example, services (DIEN) don’t create delivery documents, while physical goods do.

6) Best Practices for Custom Material Types (Z-types)

Keep it minimal: Create Z-types only when business processes differ significantly from standard types.
Reuse valuation classes: Align with existing FI account determination to avoid proliferation of G/L accounts.
Document rules: Define clear naming conventions and governance for when to use each material type.
Test integration: Ensure Z-types work across MM, SD, PP, and FI without breaking standard flows.
Avoid redundancy: Don’t duplicate standard types unless necessary; instead, adjust field selection or valuation settings.

entry.title: Oops we did it again! A brand new top-level ABAP keyword in ABAP CE 2602.
entry.content:
Introduction

New ABAP commands arise only once in most people's lifetimes. But after the ABAP command WITH in AS ABAP 7.51, we have now introduced the new ABAP command MERGE.
The fact that both keywords concern databases is no coincidence. Leveraging the full power of the HANA database is one of ABAP's highest priorities.
Note that we use "command" as a synonym for top-level keywords in this article.

Introducing a new ABAP top-level keyword in the 21st century

What is the problem with introducing a new ABAP keyword? Macros. Consider the following coding:

DEFINE merge.
  WRITE : / &1.
END-OF-DEFINITION. 
 
MERGE 'hello'.

 

After introducing MERGE as a new keyword, this code would have syntax errors. This is why, for example, RAP avoided introducing any new top-level keyword with EML. MODIFY, READ, COMMIT all existed well before RAP. 
But the developers of ABAP SQL are not afraid, as they have already shown in the past. With MERGE, they introduced the concept of a weak ABAP keyword! Macros take precedence. Here is what happens when you use a macro named MERGE in 2602:

So despite a number of warnings, nothing bad happens. You cannot use the new ABAP command MERGE in this program, though. There is and will be no escape mechanism. 

Of course, macros are not available in ABAP Cloud and this is only relevant for customers when MERGE makes it into the next On Premise release.

What does MERGE do?

MERGE is a standard SQL database command. It exists on the HANA database and most other commercial databases. Practically it is a more powerful version of UPDATE, DELETE, INSERT and MODIFY all in one command.

As MERGE is an SQL standard keyword and ABAP is a superset of SQL by tradition, we also opted to keep the command name and thus introduce a new ABAP command. 

Conceptually, MERGE is a very easy operation to understand. Here is the flow:

Here, "when no join partner found" means that the joined table (e.g., internal table) contains a set not contained in the database table. The other direction is not covered yet (see below).

 

A practical example

In this section, we give a very easy example of the MERGE command. Say you have a database table of users, and from time to time, you update it from an Excel spreadsheet stored in an internal table.  

A simple merge command would look like follows:

MERGE INTO users
  USING @Excel AS excel
  ON users~userid = excel~userid
  WHEN MATCHED AND users~managed_by_excel = @abap_true THEN UPDATE SET user_data = excel~user_data
  WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT ALL.

 This command is really self-explanatory. When you want to change the fields inserted into the database, you can also specify them as expressions in the VALUES ( .... ) clause. The usage of DELETE is fairly similar.

Some databases (for example SQL Server) have an additional clause WHEN NOT MATCHED  BY SOURCE, which is triggered when an entry is found in the joined table (e.g. excel) but not in the target table (e.g. users).  This is neither present in ABAP nor on the HANA database yet. 
Thus, conceptually, the join equals a RIGHT OUTER JOIN.

Summary

This article introduces the new ABAP command MERGE, a powerful tool for data manipulation. Due to its ability to join an internal or database table (or a global temporary table for that matter) it can be a very powerful tool in the hands of an ABAP developer.

You may find additional information in the SAP help article here.

entry.title: Strategic Data Management for S/4HANA Migration: How to Ensure a Stable and Successful Transition
entry.content:
Why Strategic Data ManagementMatters for S/4HANA Migration

S/4HANA introduces simplified data models and real-time analytics. While these improvements increase transparency and efficiency, they also expose inconsistencies that legacy systems may have tolerated.

Common issues discovered during migration include:

 
  • Duplicate vendor or customer records
  • Inconsistent product classifications
  • Incomplete historical transactions
  • Misaligned financial structures

Without a strategic data management approach, these inconsistencies disrupt operational processes and delay system stabilization.

Migration therefore becomes more than a technical activity. It becomes a structured data transformation initiative.

What Does Strategic Data Management Involve?

Strategic SAP data management focuses on ensuring that data is accurate, governed, and aligned with business processes before it enters the new environment.

Core elements include:

  • Data profiling
    Organizations analyze legacy datasets to identify inconsistencies, duplicates, and structural gaps.
  • Master data harmonization
    Customer, vendor, and material records are standardized to ensure consistency across business units.
  • Validation rules
    Business logic is applied to confirm that migrated data behaves correctly within the new system.
  • Reconciliation controls
    Financial and operational data is compared between legacy and target systems to ensure accuracy.

Together, these processes create a controlled migration environment.

Common Data Risks in S/4HANA Migration

Several risks frequently emerge when organizations underestimate data complexity.

  • Duplicate Master Data
    Legacy systems often contain multiple records representing the same business entity. In S/4HANA’s Business Partner model, such duplicates can disrupt transactions and reporting.
  • Financial Data Misalignment
    Differences in chart-of-account structures or valuation logic can produce reconciliation discrepancies after migration.
  • Inconsistent Product Data
    Variations in material master attributes may cause planning errors or inventory inconsistencies.
  • Weak Validation Controls
    Without structured validation, data may load successfully but behave incorrectly during live transactions.

Addressing these risks early significantly reduces stabilization effort.

Data Risks vs Strategic Prevention

Strategic data management helps organizations anticipate and mitigate migration challenges.

Data Risk Operational Impact Strategic Prevention
Duplicate master recordsTransaction errorsMaster data deduplication
Inconsistent material attributesProduction planning issuesData harmonization rules
Financial structure mismatchReporting discrepanciesChart-of-account alignment
Weak validation testingPost-go-live instabilityAutomated validation cycles
Poor governance ownershipSlow issue resolutionDomain-based accountability

This structured approach transforms migration from a reactive activity into a controlled process.

Case Illustration: Stabilizing an S/4HANA Migration Through Data Governance

A multinational manufacturing company preparing for S/4HANA migration encountered repeated discrepancies during early test cycles. Inventory balances did not align between systems, and duplicate vendor records caused procurement errors.

Instead of accelerating the migration timeline, the organization implemented a strategic data management program.

The program included:

  • Comprehensive data profiling across business units
  • Master data harmonization across regional systems
  • Automated validation rules during migration testing
  • Structured reconciliation checkpoints

To strengthen oversight and automate validation workflows, the company leveraged governance-driven frameworks such as DataVapte, which helped enforce Extract–Transform–Validate–Load–Reconcile controls throughout the migration lifecycle.

Within several testing cycles:

  • Inventory discrepancies decreased significantly.
  • Master data duplication was eliminated.
  • Migration stability improved ahead of go-live.

The organization successfully completed migration with minimal hypercare disruption.

The Role of Validation and Reconciliation

Validation and reconciliation are critical components of strategic data management.

Validation ensures that:

  • Data values meet business rules
  • Mandatory fields are complete
  • Structural dependencies are correct

Reconciliation confirms that:

  • Financial balances match between systems
  • Inventory quantities and values align
  • Transaction histories remain consistent

Together, these processes provide confidence that migrated data supports accurate business operations.

Governance Beyond Migration

Strategic data management should not end at go-live.

After migration, organizations must maintain governance processes that include:

  • Master data ownership assignments
  • Periodic data quality monitoring
  • Automated validation rules
  • Structured exception management

Without continuous governance, legacy issues can gradually reappear.

S/4HANA provides the opportunity to establish stronger data discipline across the enterprise.

Strategic Benefits of Strong Data Management

Organizations that invest in structured data management experience measurable advantages:

  • Operational stability
    Clean data supports reliable business processes.
  • Improved analytics
    Accurate data enables more effective decision-making.
  • Reduced remediation costs
    Preventing data issues reduces post-migration correction efforts.
  • Regulatory confidence
    Consistent data strengthens financial reporting and compliance.

Strategic data management therefore supports both operational efficiency and long-term governance.

Questions Leaders Should Ask Before Migration

Executives evaluating migration readiness should consider several questions:

  • Are master data structures standardized across business units?
  • Have validation rules been defined for key datasets?
  • Are reconciliation processes established for financial and operational data?
  • Is ownership of data governance clearly defined?
  • Are automated controls in place to monitor data quality?

Answering these questions helps ensure a stable transition to S/4HANA.

Conclusion

S/4HANA migration is often viewed as a technology transformation. In reality, it is a data transformation. Organizations that move legacy datasets without strategic oversight risk carrying operational inconsistencies into their new system.

Strategic data management provides the discipline needed to ensure a stable migration. By profiling legacy data, harmonizing master records, enforcing validation rules, and implementing governance frameworks, enterprises can transition to S/4HANA with confidence.

When migration becomes an opportunity to strengthen data integrity, organizations gain not only a new ERP system but also a more reliable foundation for analytics, automation, and long-term growth.

For more insights on SAP data governance and migration strategies, visit:
https://innovapte.com/insights

entry.title: Re: 401 Unauthorized when sending notifications from CAP...
entry.content: The credentials configured in the destination are the same ones used in Postman, taken directly from the service key. Since the request works correctly in Postman using these credentials, it seems unlikely that the issue is caused by incorrect credentials in the destination. Therefore, it appears the problem may be related to how the destination or authentication flow is being handled by the application rather than the credentials themselves.

entry.title: SAP — серед ТОП-50 AI-лідерів України! 🚀
entry.content:
SAP увійшла до національного рейтингу ТОП-50 AI-лідерів України від ділового видання DELO.ua у категорії IT, підтверджуючи статус компанії як одного з ключових драйверів впровадження штучного інтелекту в українському бізнесі.​

Рейтинг DELO.ua відзначає компанії, що не просто експериментують з AI, а інтегрують його в основу бізнес-стратегії, операцій і управлінських рішень. Для SAP це визнання того, що штучний інтелект уже став частиною цифрової архітектури рішень і допомагає компаніям підвищувати ефективність, автоматизувати процеси та створювати нові цінні сервіси.

Центральним елементом AI-екосистеми SAP є генеративний асистент Joule, інтегрований у хмарні рішення компанії. Він допомагає працювати з бізнес-даними в контексті, надає релевантні рекомендації, прискорює ухвалення рішень та відкриває нові сценарії автоматизації для фінансів, логістики, HR та інших напрямів.

SAP також розвиває власні AI-моделі, орієнтовані на роботу з бізнес-даними — від прогнозування ключових показників до аналізу великих масивів інформації й прискорення розробки корпоративних цифрових рішень. Усе це формує концепцію SAP Business AI, яка дозволяє вбудовувати інтелектуальні можливості безпосередньо в щоденні процеси компаній.

Чи плануєте ви вже цього року запуск або масштабування AI-проєктів на базі рішень SAP у своїй компанії?

entry.title: Re: Valuation Level Configuration
entry.content:
Hello @easap ,

 

Concept / QuestionExplanation, Business Impact & SAP Notes
What is Valuation Level in SAP?Valuation level determines where material valuation is maintained in the organizational structure. It controls whether material prices and inventory values are stored per plant or per company code.
What is Valuation Area?The valuation area is the organizational unit where material valuation occurs. Depending on configuration, the valuation area can be either: • Plant (most common) • Company Code
Plant-level valuationEach plant has its own material price and inventory value. Same material can have different valuation in different plants.
Company code-level valuationMaterial valuation is maintained once per company code. All plants within the company code share the same material price and inventory value.
Where this is configuredConfiguration path: SPRO → Materials Management → Valuation and Account Assignment → Define Valuation Level
Why SAP allows two optionsDifferent businesses manage inventory costs differently: • decentralized manufacturing companies require plant-level costing • centralized purchasing organizations prefer company code valuation
Impact on material masterIf plant-level valuation is used: • Accounting view fields depend on plant • Material price stored per plant in MBEW table
Impact on accountingPlant-level valuation enables separate inventory values and cost of goods sold per plant, improving cost transparency. Company code-level valuation aggregates inventory valuation across plants.
Impact on controlling and profitabilityPlant-level valuation supports detailed cost tracking, transfer pricing between plants, and plant-level profitability analysis.
Impact on inventory movementsWith plant-level valuation, inventory transfers between plants within the same company code may create accounting postings if valuation differs.
Impact on organizational structureChoosing plant-level valuation aligns with decentralized production or distribution networks. Company code-level valuation suits simpler structures with centralized costing.
Technical tables involved• T001K – valuation area configuration • MBEW – material valuation data • T030 – automatic account determination
Standard SAP recommendationSAP recommends plant-level valuation because it provides better flexibility for cost management and is required for many advanced processes (e.g., transfer pricing, material ledger).
SAP Help ReferenceSAP Help Portal → Material Valuation in SAP S/4HANA SAP Help → Valuation Areas and Organizational Structure
Relevant SAP Notes (Verify Applicability)📌 SAP Note 307648 – Valuation Area Concept in MM 📌 SAP Note 157804 – Material Valuation Configuration Logic 📌 SAP Note 1916010 – Account Determination and Valuation Areas

Example Scenario

Plant-Level Valuation

Material A100

Plant 1000 €10
Plant 2000 €12

Inventory values differ between plants.

Company Code-Level Valuation

Material A100

Company Code Price
1000 €11

All plants use the same valuation.

Business Decision Impact

-Plant valuation - High cost transparency, complex configuration
-Company code valuation - Simpler accounting, less cost detail


Remmember, once valuation level is defined and productive postings exist, it cannot be changed without system reconfiguration or migration.

Recommendation

Most S/4HANA implementations use plant-level valuation because it supports:

-Material Ledger
-Transfer pricing
-Parallel valuations
-Advanced manufacturing costing

If you find my response helpful, share your feedback. This motivates me to serve you better.
Appreciate your support.
Thanks,
Divyesh

entry.title: Re: Report RFBILA10 - Button "Data Sources for G/LAccount...
entry.content:
Hello @Jan_-1 ,

Your archive data is correct, but RFBILA10 likely does not activate archive access due to missing archive information structures or logical database initialization conditions.
Many classic FI reports behave differently after S/4HANA migration, especially when they rely on logical databases instead of CDS views, which can affect archive access initialization.

Troubleshoot

-Go to SARJ

Check archive information structures for FI_DOCUMNT / FI_ACCOUNT
Ensure they are built and active

-Go to SARI → Customizing

Confirm logical database SDF is assigned to those archive objects.

-Rebuild archive information structures if needed.

Test again in RFBILA10 with archive access.

I cannot think of other possibilities. Hopefully it works.

If you find my response helpful, share your feedback. This motivates me to serve you better.
Appreciate your support.
Thanks,
Divyesh

entry.title: Re: GIT Aging report
entry.content:
Hello @Kasirajan 

Thank you for your question.

If your requirement is to build a GIT (Goods in Transit) Aging Report in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, then yes, it is possible to achieve this using Embedded Analytics with SAP Analytics Cloud. However, there is no standard delivered report in Public Cloud that provides a detailed aging analysis specifically for GIT stock.

To meet this requirement, the recommended approach is to build a custom analytical report using Embedded SAC.

The typical solution involves the following steps.

First, create a Custom CDS View using the Custom CDS Views app.
You can select stock-related data sources such as:

I_MaterialStock_2 – Material Stock

C_MaterialStockActual – Total Actual Stock

These CDS views provide stock quantities and posting-date-based inventory information required for aging calculations.

You can also add associations such as:

I_Product – to retrieve Material Group and Product details

I_InventoryPriceByKeyDate_2 – to retrieve inventory valuation information

In the CDS view, include dimensions like:

Plant

Material

Material Group

Supplier

Batch

Storage Location

Add measures such as:

Stock Quantity

Inventory Price

Inventory Value

You should also create a Key Date input parameter so that aging can be calculated based on a specific date. Typically, the CDS view includes a filter such as:

Posting Date ≤ Key Date

After creating and publishing the CDS view (for example YY1_GIT_STOCK_AGING), the next step is to create a Custom Analytical Query using the Custom Analytical Queries app.

This analytical query (for example YY1_GIT_STOCK_AGING_Q) will consume the CDS view and define:

Dimensions

Plant

Material

Material Group

Supplier

Batch

Storage Location

Measures

Stock Quantity

Inventory Price

Total Inventory Amount

You can also define calculated measures such as:

ZInventoryPrice – latest inventory price using exception aggregation = Last by Posting Date

ZTotalAmount – calculated inventory value (Quantity × Price)

Next, create an SAC Story using the Manage KPIs and Reports app.

Select the analytical query YY1_GIT_STOCK_AGING_Q as the data source and design a table-based report with aging buckets, such as:

0–30 days

31–60 days

61–90 days

91–120 days

121–180 days

181–360 days

361–730 days

730 days

Each bucket is created as a restricted measure based on posting date relative to the key date.

Finally, publish the story as a tile on the SAP Fiori Launchpad using the Custom Catalog Extensions app so that business users can easily access the report.

With this approach, customers can monitor:

Quantity of goods in transit

Aging distribution of GIT inventory

Inventory value by aging bucket

Potential supply chain delays

Because this solution uses Embedded SAC within S/4HANA Cloud, it provides flexible analytics and interactive dashboards while remaining fully integrated with operational data.

You can also refer to the following SAP Help documentation for more details about Embedded Analytics and SAC integration:

SAP S/4HANA Cloud – Embedded Analytics

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a630d57fc5004c6383e7a81efee7a8bb/dd28bf545e91ee05e10000000a4450e5.html

SAP Analytics Cloud Integration with S/4HANA Cloud

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/93bd0b1e72ca4cbcbfb942d4497529f7/268ba92ba79b4ad5803044edf524e42b.html

Custom CDS Views in S/4HANA Cloud
https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0f69f8fb28ac4bf48d2b57b9637e81fa/e30de6eae4d24d70b65996ac8ff88848.html

If your scenario involves intercompany stock transfers or STO processes generating GIT stock, the same analytical approach can be applied by selecting the appropriate stock CDS views and movement data.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

HM Awasthi

entry.title: Re: GIT Aging report
entry.content:
Dear @Kasirajan,

There is no SAP-delivered standard report specifically documented with aging buckets for goods in transit. SAP documents two related standard capabilities instead inventory analytics to monitor stock aging in general and analysis on transfer posting which provides an overview of overdue materials for which a stock transport order has been created. The closest standard app for this requirement is Overdue Materials Stock in Transit. SAP explicitly documents this app for monitoring overdue stock transport order materials and SAP also recommends it in the stock transfer procedure documentation

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/aff0b3f5f46c42a2b2c0fabfc233bab2/4394fcca1adf4009bf33fa864e497613.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/32da8359c8ee4e8b8e8c5e15cacba5aa/9993b853dcfcb44ce10000000a174cb4.html

For quantity visibility standard inventory reporting can show stock that is in transfer/transit. SAP documents that if transit stocks or stock transfer stocks exist at plant level they are displayed in the In Stock Transfer column in stock reporting. However this is stock visibility, not a true aging-by-bucket report

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/32da8359c8ee4e8b8e8c5e15cacba5aa/5863bd534f22b44ce10000000a174cb4.html

Summary:

SAP in Public Cloud does not provide a dedicated standard GIT aging report. If the business needs a real aging analysis for GIT for example 0–30 / 31–60 / 61+ days in transit this would typically need to be built as a custom report using Custom CDS Views and Custom Analytical Queries

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0f69f8fb28ac4bf48d2b57b9637e81fa/e30de6eae4d24d70b65996ac8ff88848.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0f69f8fb28ac4bf48d2b57b9637e81fa/33f3a229ecfe438ca6b2053c48b4e1bb.html

For example:

  • use I_PurchaseOrderItemAPI01 as the STO base
  • add I_PurchaseOrderHistoryAPI01 if you need execution/history context
  • use I_StockQuantityCurrentValue or I_MaterialStockTimeSeries for the stock side
  • then calculate Days in Transit and bucket it in Custom Analytical Queries. SAP documents that Custom CDS Views support associations, filters and calculated fields and Custom Analytical Queries support filters, restricted measures and calculated measures

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Purchase Order Item Related Delivery address with Cou...
entry.content:
Hi,

Please refer to Purchase Order Item | SAP Help Portal and available CDS views CDS Views for Purchase Orders | SAP Help Portal

For SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, the delivery address at the PO item level is linked via the Delivery Address ID in the PO item. The standard released CDS view I_PurchaseOrderItemAPI01 exposes the relevant fields:

  • DeliveryAddressCountry – Country of the delivery address

  • DeliveryAddressRegion – Region/State of the delivery address

These fields can be used directly in your custom PO output for tax or reporting purposes. If additional address details are required (street, city, or full region description), the PO item’s Delivery Address ID can be joined to the I_ADDRESS and optionally I_REGION CDS views to fetch complete address information. This approach ensures accurate retrieval of tax-relevant country and region information at the item level for your custom output forms.

Thanks and regards,

SAP Support

entry.title: Re: How to expose sap-abap-1 model with LiteLLM Proxy ?
entry.content:
@Cocquerel 

You need to explicitly tell LiteLLM that this model should be routed through the SAP AI Core provider (or whichever specific integration you are using) by adding the litellm_params block to your model definition.

Update your config.yaml as follows:

model_list:
  - model_name: sap-abap-1  # The name you want to use in your API calls
    litellm_params:
      model: sap/sap-abap-1 # The actual model string including the provider prefix
      # If 'sap/' prefix isn't enough, explicitly set the custom_llm_provider
      custom_llm_provider: openai 
      api_base: "YOUR_SAP_AI_CORE_BASE_URL" # Usually defined in your env, but can be explicit here

Why this is happening

  1. Missing Prefix: LiteLLM uses prefixes (like anthropic/, gemini/, openai/) to route requests. If you just pass sap-abap-1, it looks for a provider named "sap-abap-1", fails to find it, and throws the BadRequestError.

  2. Provider Mapping: For SAP AI Core specifically, LiteLLM often expects the model to be defined using its OpenAI-compatible proxy interface.

Recommended Configuration Structure

If you are following the SAP Agentic Examples documentation, your config.yaml should look like this to ensure all models are recognized correctly:

 
 
model_list:
  - model_name: anthropic--claude-4.5-sonnet
    litellm_params:
      model: sap/anthropic--claude-4.5-sonnet
  
  - model_name: gemini-2.5-pro
    litellm_params:
      model: sap/gemini-2.5-pro

  - model_name: sap-abap-1
    litellm_params:
      model: sap/sap-abap-1  # Ensure the 'sap/' prefix is present here

Steps to Verify

  1. Add the sap/ prefix: In the litellm_params: model: section, ensure the model string starts with sap/.

  2. Environment Variables: Ensure your SAP AI Core credentials (AICORE_AUTH_URL, AICORE_CLIENT_ID, AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET, and AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP) are correctly loaded in the environment where the Proxy is running.

  3. Restart the Proxy: After changing the YAML, restart the LiteLLM proxy to reload the deployment list.

If the error persists even with the sap/ prefix, it means the version of LiteLLM you are using might not have the SAP provider logic updated for that specific model name. In that case, adding custom_llm_provider: openai inside litellm_params is the standard fallback for SAP AI Core models.

entry.title: Re: Why is help.sap.com not accessible to ai agents?
entry.content:
TL;DR: It doesn't. It block pages that are decades out of date. 

Hello @christian_noack ,

Not an SAP employee, but I think I have an answer for you.

Are agents allowed to read help.sap.com?

Yes. 

The robots.txt file contains restrictions like :

User-agent: ChatGPT-User
Allow: /
Disallow: /*/frameset.htm
Disallow: /doc/*.htm$

That means that ChatGPT-User can read everything, except files that looks like this:

https://help.sap.com/docs/7d68f1a4.htm

$ = anchors the end of the URL (path + optional query, depending on the crawler). With $, the match must end right there.

The htm extension is ancient history by this point, but back in the 90s, operating systems didn't support an extension more than three letters. 
Note, SAP has been in business since the 80s, so it's not unthinkable that some .htm files still exist ✨somewhere✨, and by now they'll be extremely out of date. 
Robots.txt is used for SEO as well as bots, so you can imagine why SAP doesn't want a user-guide from 1992 showing up in search results. 

So, the site may have originally had .htm page, and after switching to .html, they wanted to make sure only new documents were indexed. 

"In these cases the information I get is often supar and I don't know of an alternative"

That being said, yes - I think a lot of people will agree that AI in does not answer SAP questions very well, even though it has access to the documentation.

AI would be god awful if it only had access to the official Python documentation, because it's very general and doesn't solve any one problem in particular. What makes AI good at Python is the absolute landslide of articles, blogs, git hub repos, etc. of Python projects that the AI has been able to index. 

The answers to your questions are subpar, simply because there is only a fraction of the material available for SAP products, compared to other software solutions.

"...what should I do instead?"

Well, I don't mean to be rude at all, but you could read the documentation?
It's a bit like asking

"a book I'm interested in is not available as an audiobook? What should I do?"

You'll just have to read the book yourself. 

Again, I'm not trying to be a dick, but I'm not really sure what else to say.

entry.title: Common Inventory Audit Challenges During Stock Taking
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

We are preparing for our periodic physical inventory (stock taking) and would like to understand the common challenges organizations face during inventory audits.

Some issues we are currently concerned about include:

  • Differences between system stock and physical stock
  • Counting errors during manual stock taking
  • Incorrect storage locations or bin issues
  • Handling damaged, obsolete, or expired materials
  • Stock in transit during the audit period
  • Operational disruptions during counting
  • Data entry or posting delays

We would appreciate insights on:

  • What are the most common inventory audit challenges in real-world scenarios?
  • Best practices to minimize discrepancies during stock taking
  • Recommended controls or procedures to ensure audit accuracy
  • Any SAP-specific tips for physical inventory processes

Thank you in advance for your guidance.

Best regards,
Stockount

entry.title: SAP Generative AI Hub: RAG on SAP Data with HANA Cloud Vector Store (Part 4 of 6)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  SERIES: PART 4 OF 6  |  SAP AI CORE  |  HANA CLOUD  |  RAG

SAP Generative AI Hub: RAG on SAP Data with HANA Cloud Vector Store

End-to-end RAG pipeline on SAP data — REAL_VECTOR schema setup, SAP embedding model, document chunking, similarity search, and four working use cases: knowledge base search, data dictionary retrieval, transport log search, and master data context retrieval.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  Part 4 of 6  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: HANA Cloud's native REAL_VECTOR data type turns your existing SAP database into a vector store — no separate infrastructure required. Combined with the SAP embedding model through AI Core and the Generative AI Hub, you get a fully BTP-native RAG pipeline on your SAP data. This article covers the complete path: schema setup, embedding generation, chunking strategy, similarity search, and four SAP-specific RAG use cases with working code.

Series Context

This is Part 4 of a 6-part series on SAP Generative AI Hub. Parts 1–3 covered setup, Python SDK integration, and prompt pipelines. This part connects those pipelines to persistent SAP data through retrieval-augmented generation — the pattern that makes foundation model outputs grounded in your actual SAP context rather than general training data.

RAG is the architectural bridge between the prompt pipelines from Part 3 and the production SAP applications coming in Parts 5 and 6. Understanding it here makes the CAP and Joule integration articles that follow significantly easier to follow.

WHY RAG FOR SAP WORKLOADS 

Why Foundation Models Need RAG in SAP Contexts

Foundation models — even the most capable ones — have no knowledge of your SAP landscape. They do not know your custom Z-tables, your transport history, your material master conventions, your configured process variants, or the accumulated institutional knowledge in your SAP documentation. When you ask a foundation model an SAP question without context, it answers from general training data. That is not good enough for enterprise use.

RAG solves this by retrieving relevant context from your own SAP data at query time and passing it to the model alongside the question. The model's answer is grounded in your actual system — your data, your configuration, your history.

HANA Cloud makes this particularly clean for SAP practitioners because the vector store lives in the same database as your SAP data. There is no separate vector database to provision, secure, or maintain. Your embeddings sit alongside your operational data in HANA, with the same access controls, backup policies, and compliance posture you already manage.

SAP data sources → Chunk + embed → Store in HANA REAL_VECTOR → Query similarity search → Grounded model response

STEP 1: HANA VECTOR STORE SCHEMA SETUP 

Creating the REAL_VECTOR Schema in HANA Cloud

What REAL_VECTOR Is

REAL_VECTOR is a native HANA Cloud data type introduced for vector storage. It stores fixed-dimension floating-point arrays — the numerical representations (embeddings) that capture the semantic meaning of text. HANA's vector engine supports cosine similarity and L2 distance calculations directly in SQL, which means your similarity search runs inside the database without moving data out.

REAL_VECTOR is a HANA Cloud feature — it is not available in on-premise SAP HANA. Confirm your HANA Cloud instance version supports vector engine capabilities before proceeding. As of 2026, vector store functionality requires HANA Cloud QRC 1/2024 or later.

Schema Creation

Create a dedicated schema for your vector store tables. Keeping vector store tables separate from operational SAP data makes access control and maintenance significantly cleaner.

-- Create dedicated schema for vector store
CREATE SCHEMA AI_VECTOR_STORE;
 
-- General purpose document embedding table
CREATE TABLE AI_VECTOR_STORE.DOCUMENT_EMBEDDINGS (
    EMBEDDING_ID    NVARCHAR(100)   NOT NULL PRIMARY KEY,
    SOURCE_TYPE     NVARCHAR(50)    NOT NULL,  -- e.g. 'KB', 'DDIC', 'TRANSPORT'
    SOURCE_ID       NVARCHAR(200)   NOT NULL,  -- original record identifier
    CHUNK_INDEX     INTEGER         NOT NULL,  -- chunk position within source doc
    CHUNK_TEXT      NCLOB           NOT NULL,  -- original text of this chunk
    EMBEDDING       REAL_VECTOR(1536),         -- embedding vector (1536 dims)
    METADATA        NCLOB,                     -- JSON metadata for filtering
    CREATED_AT      TIMESTAMP       DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);
 
-- Index for fast source type filtering before vector search
CREATE INDEX IDX_DOC_EMB_SOURCE ON AI_VECTOR_STORE.DOCUMENT_EMBEDDINGS (SOURCE_TYPE, SOURCE_ID);

The EMBEDDING column dimension (1536) must match the output dimension of your embedding model exactly. The SAP embedding model used in this series outputs 1536-dimensional vectors. If you change embedding models, you must recreate the table with the correct dimension — existing embeddings cannot be reused with a different model.

STEP 2: GENERATING EMBEDDINGS VIA AI CORE 

Generating Embeddings with the SAP Embedding Model

Why the SAP Embedding Model

The SAP embedding model available through AI Core's Generative AI Hub is tuned for enterprise and business content — the kind of domain-specific language that appears in SAP documentation, functional specs, transport descriptions, and master data. For SAP-specific RAG applications, it consistently outperforms general-purpose embedding models on retrieval precision for SAP terminology and process language.

Using it through the Hub also keeps the entire pipeline within the BTP trust boundary — the same governance, audit logging, and data residency controls that apply to your generative AI calls apply to embedding generation too.

Document Chunking Strategy

Chunking — splitting source documents into segments before embedding — is one of the most consequential decisions in a RAG pipeline. Chunks that are too large dilute the embedding signal. Chunks that are too small lose context. For SAP content, the following strategy produced the best retrieval results across our four use cases:

 

Source Type

Chunk Size

Chunking Approach

SAP Knowledge Base articles

500–700 tokens per chunk

Paragraph boundaries — keep procedural steps together

HANA data dictionary descriptions

Full field/table description as single chunk

Short content — no chunking needed

Transport / change log entries

One transport record per chunk

Natural boundary — each transport is self-contained

Master data descriptions

One record per chunk with key fields as prefix

Prefix with MATNR/LIFNR for retrieval context

Embedding Generation Code

# Step 2: Generate embeddings using SAP embedding model via AI Core
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.native.openai import OpenAI
import hdbcli.dbapi as hdb
import json, uuid
 
# Initialize SDK client — credentials from environment or config file
client = OpenAI()
 
# Connect to HANA Cloud
conn = hdb.connect(
    address='<your-hana-host>.hanacloud.ondemand.com',
    port=443, user='<user>', password='<password>', encrypt=True
)
 
def generate_embedding(text: str) -> list:
    """Generate embedding vector using SAP embedding model via AI Core."""
    response = client.embeddings.create(
        model='text-embedding-ada-002',
        # SAP-provided embedding model — routed through AI Core
        input=text
    )
    return response.data[0].embedding
 
def chunk_text(text: str, chunk_size: int = 600, overlap: int = 50) -> list:
    """Split text into overlapping chunks by word count."""
    words = text.split()
    chunks = []
    for i in range(0, len(words), chunk_size - overlap):
        chunk = ' '.join(words[i:i + chunk_size])
        if chunk:
            chunks.append(chunk)
    return chunks
 
def store_embeddings(source_type: str, source_id: str,
                     text: str, metadata: dict = None):
    """Chunk, embed, and store a document in HANA vector store."""
    chunks = chunk_text(text)
    cursor = conn.cursor()
 
    for idx, chunk in enumerate(chunks):
        embedding = generate_embedding(chunk)
        embedding_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
 
        cursor.execute('''
            INSERT INTO AI_VECTOR_STORE.DOCUMENT_EMBEDDINGS
            (EMBEDDING_ID, SOURCE_TYPE, SOURCE_ID, CHUNK_INDEX,
             CHUNK_TEXT, EMBEDDING, METADATA)
            VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?), ?)
        ''', (
            embedding_id, source_type, source_id, idx,
            chunk, str(embedding),
            json.dumps(metadata) if metadata else None
        ))
 
    conn.commit()
    print(f'Stored {len(chunks)} chunks for {source_type}/{source_id}')

Embedding generation has a cost per token through AI Core. For initial ingestion of large SAP document sets, run embedding generation in batches with rate limiting and store results immediately — do not regenerate embeddings on every pipeline run. Re-embed only when source documents change.

STEP 3: SIMILARITY SEARCH IN HANA SQL 

Querying the Vector Store with COSINE_SIMILARITY

How HANA Vector Search Works

HANA Cloud's vector engine computes similarity between a query embedding and all stored embeddings directly in SQL using built-in functions. COSINE_SIMILARITY measures the angle between vectors — returning values between -1 and 1, where 1 is identical. For SAP document retrieval, cosine similarity consistently outperforms L2 distance because it is scale-invariant — document length differences do not artificially inflate distance scores.

Similarity Search Function

# Step 3: Similarity search — retrieve top-k most relevant chunks
def similarity_search(query: str, source_type: str = None,
                      top_k: int = 5) -> list:
    """
    Embed query and retrieve most similar chunks from HANA vector store.
    Optionally filter by source_type for targeted retrieval.
    """
    query_embedding = generate_embedding(query)
    cursor = conn.cursor()
 
    # Build query with optional source type filter
    source_filter = "AND SOURCE_TYPE = ?" if source_type else ""
    params = [str(query_embedding), top_k]
    if source_type:
        params.insert(1, source_type)
 
    cursor.execute(f'''
        SELECT TOP ?
            SOURCE_TYPE,
            SOURCE_ID,
            CHUNK_INDEX,
            CHUNK_TEXT,
            METADATA,
            COSINE_SIMILARITY(EMBEDDING, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?)) AS SIMILARITY_SCORE
        FROM AI_VECTOR_STORE.DOCUMENT_EMBEDDINGS
        WHERE 1=1 {source_filter}
        ORDER BY SIMILARITY_SCORE DESC
    ''', params)
 
    results = []
    for row in cursor.fetchall():
        results.append({
            'source_type': row[0],
            'source_id':   row[1],
            'chunk_index': row[2],
            'chunk_text':  row[3],
            'metadata':    json.loads(row[4]) if row[4] else {},
            'score':       float(row[5])
        })
    return results

Filter by SOURCE_TYPE before running vector similarity whenever you know which data domain is relevant to the query. Narrowing the search space improves both retrieval precision and query performance — especially as your vector store grows across multiple SAP data domains.

STEP 4: THE COMPLETE RAG PIPELINE 

Assembling the End-to-End RAG Pipeline

With embedding generation and similarity search in place, the RAG pipeline itself is straightforward: embed the query, retrieve relevant chunks, assemble them as context, and pass context plus query to the foundation model. The model's response is grounded in your retrieved SAP data.

# Step 4: Complete RAG pipeline function
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
 
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='claude-3.5-sonnet')
 
def rag_query(question: str, source_type: str = None,
              top_k: int = 5, system_context: str = None) -> str:
    """
    Full RAG pipeline: retrieve relevant SAP context,
    then generate grounded response using foundation model.
    """
    # Step 1: Retrieve relevant context from HANA vector store
    retrieved = similarity_search(question, source_type, top_k)
 
    # Step 2: Assemble context from retrieved chunks
    context_blocks = []
    for r in retrieved:
        context_blocks.append(
            f"[Source: {r['source_type']} / {r['source_id']} ",
            f"| Score: {r['score']:.3f}]\n{r['chunk_text']}"
        )
    context = '\n\n---\n\n'.join(context_blocks)
 
    # Step 3: Build grounded prompt with retrieved context
    default_system = '''You are an SAP expert assistant.
    Answer questions using ONLY the context provided below.
    If the context does not contain enough information to answer,
    say so explicitly — do not use general knowledge.
    Always reference the source when citing specific information.'''
 
    messages = [
        SystemMessage(content=system_context or default_system),
        HumanMessage(content=f'''
CONTEXT FROM SAP DATA:\n{context}
\n\nQUESTION: {question}''')
    ]
 
    # Step 4: Generate grounded response
    return llm(messages).content

The instruction 'Answer using ONLY the context provided — do not use general knowledge' is critical for SAP RAG pipelines. Without it, the model supplements retrieved context with training data, which can introduce information about standard SAP that contradicts your specific configuration or customizations.

FOUR SAP RAG USE CASES 

Use Case Examples

  ▶  Use Case 1: SAP Knowledge Base Search 

Grounded Q&A on SAP Documentation

Ingest your SAP knowledge base articles, configuration guides, and internal runbooks into the vector store. The RAG pipeline then answers practitioner questions grounded in your actual documented procedures — not generic SAP guidance from the model's training data.

# Ingest SAP knowledge base articles
kb_articles = [
    {
        'id': 'KB-2024-001',
        'title': 'HANA Delta Load: Handling Large Volume Tables',
        'content': '''When delta loading tables exceeding 500M records...
        Use hash-based partitioning with MOD(ABS(TO_BIGINT(KEY_FIELD)), N)
        to distribute load across parallel workers. Monitor memory via
        M_HEAP_MEMORY view. Control table must track per-partition watermarks.'''
    },
]
 
for article in kb_articles:
    store_embeddings(
        source_type='KB',
        source_id=article['id'],
        text=f"{article['title']}\n\n{article['content']}",
        metadata={'title': article['title']}
    )
 
# Query the knowledge base
answer = rag_query(
    question='How should I handle integer overflow in HANA delta loads?',
    source_type='KB',
    top_k=3
)
print(answer)

  ▶  Use Case 2: HANA Data Dictionary Search 

Natural Language Queries on Table and Field Metadata

Embed HANA data dictionary metadata — table descriptions, field definitions, domain values, foreign key relationships — so practitioners can find relevant tables and fields using natural language instead of needing to know exact technical names upfront.

# Ingest HANA data dictionary metadata from HANA system views
cursor = conn.cursor()
cursor.execute('''
    SELECT T.TABLE_NAME, T.COMMENTS,
           STRING_AGG(C.COLUMN_NAME || ': ' || IFNULL(C.COMMENTS,''),  ', ')
           AS FIELD_LIST
    FROM SYS.TABLES T
    JOIN SYS.TABLE_COLUMNS C ON T.TABLE_NAME = C.TABLE_NAME
    WHERE T.SCHEMA_NAME = 'SAPHANADB'
    AND T.TABLE_NAME LIKE 'Z%'
    -- Scope to your custom Z-tables first
    GROUP BY T.TABLE_NAME, T.COMMENTS
    LIMIT 500
''')
 
for row in cursor.fetchall():
    table_name, description, fields = row
    content = f'Table: {table_name}\nDescription: {description}\nFields: {fields}'
    store_embeddings(
        source_type='DDIC',
        source_id=table_name,
        text=content,
        metadata={'table_name': table_name}
    )
 
# Find relevant tables using natural language
answer = rag_query(
    question='Which tables store customer payment terms and credit limits?',
    source_type='DDIC',
    top_k=5
)
print(answer)

  ▶  Use Case 3: Transport and Change Log Retrieval 

Semantic Search Across SAP Change History

Transport request descriptions and change logs are high-value institutional knowledge that is almost always searched by keyword — missing semantic connections between related changes. Embedding transport history enables semantic retrieval: find transports related to a concept or problem even when the exact terms don't match.

# Ingest transport descriptions from E070 / E07T
cursor.execute('''
    SELECT T.TRKORR, T.AS4TEXT, T.AS4DATE, T.AS4USER,
           STRING_AGG(O.OBJECT || '/' || O.OBJ_NAME, ', ') AS OBJECTS
    FROM E070 T
    JOIN E071 O ON T.TRKORR = O.TRKORR
    WHERE T.AS4DATE >= '20250101'
    AND T.TRSTATUS = 'R'
    -- Released transports only
    GROUP BY T.TRKORR, T.AS4TEXT, T.AS4DATE, T.AS4USER
''')
 
for row in cursor.fetchall():
    tr_id, description, date, user, objects = row
    content = f'''Transport: {tr_id}
Description: {description}
Changed by: {user} on {date}
Objects: {objects}'''
    store_embeddings(
        source_type='TRANSPORT',
        source_id=tr_id,
        text=content,
        metadata={'tr_id': tr_id, 'date': str(date), 'user': user}
    )
 
# Semantic search across transport history
answer = rag_query(
    question='What changes were made to the FI posting handler in 2025?',
    source_type='TRANSPORT',
    top_k=5
)
print(answer)

  ▶  Use Case 4: Master Data Context Retrieval for AI Classification 

Retrieval-Augmented Master Data Classification

This use case combines the master data classification pipeline from Part 3 with RAG — retrieving similar previously-classified records as few-shot examples at query time. The model classifies new records guided by real examples from your own SAP master data, not generic training examples. This produces significantly higher accuracy on company-specific classification schemes.

# Ingest already-classified master data records as reference examples
cursor.execute('''
    SELECT MATNR, MAKTX, MATKL, MTART, ZZCATEGORY
    FROM MARA JOIN MAKT ON MARA.MATNR = MAKT.MATNR
    WHERE MAKT.SPRAS = 'E'
    AND ZZCATEGORY IS NOT NULL
    -- Only records with confirmed classifications
    LIMIT 1000
''')
 
for row in cursor.fetchall():
    matnr, desc, matkl, mtart, category = row
    content = f'Material: {matnr} | {desc} | Type: {mtart} | Category: {category}'
    store_embeddings(
        source_type='MASTER_DATA',
        source_id=matnr,
        text=content,
        metadata={'matnr': matnr, 'category': category, 'mtart': mtart}
    )
 
# Classify new material using retrieved similar records as examples
new_material = 'Hydraulic seal kit 250bar SAP-HYD-250'
 
answer = rag_query(
    question=f'Classify this new material: {new_material}',
    source_type='MASTER_DATA',
    top_k=5,
    system_context='''You are an SAP master data classifier.
    Use the similar records provided as classification examples.
    Return JSON only: {"category": "...", "confidence": "High/Medium/Low",
    "based_on": [list of similar MATNR values used as reference]}'''
)
print(answer)

Retrieval-augmented classification using your own historical master data consistently outperforms zero-shot classification because the model is guided by your specific category labels and conventions — not generic industry taxonomies. The more classified records you ingest, the more accurate the retrieval and the better the classification output.

DESIGN CONSIDERATIONS 

What to Get Right Before Going to Production

 

Consideration

Guidance

Embedding consistency

Never mix embedding models across records in the same table. A similarity search comparing vectors from different models produces meaningless scores. Pick one model and stick to it — if you change models, re-embed everything.

Chunk overlap

50-token overlap between chunks prevents relevant context from being split across chunk boundaries. Adjust overlap up for dense technical content like ABAP code or configuration tables.

Score thresholds

Not all retrieved chunks are relevant. Filter results below a COSINE_SIMILARITY score of 0.75 before passing to the model. Low-score chunks add noise and reduce answer quality.

Re-embedding cadence

SAP data changes. Transport descriptions, knowledge base articles, and master data classifications go stale. Build a delta re-embedding job that runs on the same schedule as your delta data loads.

Source type filtering

Always filter by SOURCE_TYPE when the query domain is known. Mixing transport history chunks with knowledge base chunks in a single retrieval pass produces lower precision than targeted retrieval from the right domain.

What's Next in This Series

  • Part 5: Integrating AI Core into a BTP CAP Application — production-grade AI with OData APIs, S/4HANA integration, and the RAG pipeline from this article embedded in a CAP service
  • Part 6: Extending SAP Joule with Foundation Models — custom Joule skills backed by AI Core, with HANA vector store retrieval for grounded natural language interaction across SAP applications

The vector store you build in this article carries forward into Parts 5 and 6. If you're following the series end-to-end, the RAG pipeline here is the foundation that makes the CAP application and Joule extension in the next two parts genuinely useful rather than demo-grade. Drop a comment if you hit issues with REAL_VECTOR column dimensions or similarity score thresholds — these are the two most common friction points in production deployments.

entry.title: Re: Scrapping / Issuance of Expired Batches
entry.content:
Keep the message as it is an Error. Go to tcode OMJ5 and make the entry for movement type 551 = 3 (no check at goods issue).

This will allow you to scrap the expired stock while still preventing other movements on the stock.

entry.title: Re: Manufacturer Part Number; not visible in Purchase Req...
entry.content:
Dear @Adbo,

In Public Cloud the recommended solution is to make Manufacturer Part Number (MPN) available in the purchase requisition through in-app extensibility. SAP documents MPN as part of the standard product master data model under ProductManufacturerNumber and SAP also documents Manufacturer part number as a supported attribute in the purchase requisition item integration model. This confirms that MPN is available as a standard manufacturer-related attribute in the Public Cloud data model and can be used in the purchase requisition integration model

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/bb9f1469daf04bd894ab2167f8132a1a/f20f62544f6b4188b55b913b47ccd118.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/3c916ef10fc240c9afc594b346ffaf77/2c973258fb9d2060e10000000a44147b.html

For the purchase requisition apps, SAP supported Public Cloud approach is to use Custom Fields with business context Procurement Purchase Requisition Item. SAP purchasing extensibility guide shows that the custom field can be enabled on the Purchase Requisition Item exposed in API/CDS and transferred further from Purchase Requisition to Purchase Order

 

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/9e9ea1e208a14d2d8e6274ffc4c3fd84.html

So:

1. Create a custom field such as Manufacturer Part Number in Custom Fields app for business context Procurement Purchase Requisition Item

2. Enable it for the PR UI and, if needed for reporting/API usage

3. Default or validate the field automatically based on the selected product/material using the released PR extensibility options. SAP documents the PR item enhancement option MM_PUR_S4_PR_MODIFY_ITEM for checking or changing values at requisition item level

This gives requisitioners direct visibility of the correct Manufacturer Part Number during PR creation, improves material precision and keeps the logic clearly separated from Supplier Part Number. It is also aligned with the clean-core approach for Public Cloud

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/720dcec59d264114bc4ec19759aa4514.html

https://fioriappslibrary.hana.ondemand.com/sap/fix/externalViewer/#/detail/Apps('F1481')/S36

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Issue with Balance Carryforward and Legacy Data Transfer Settings (CoCode Status & Fiscal Years)
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

We are currently performing the fiscal year-end closing for 2025 in an S/4HANA Public Cloud environment. Our Go-Live/Legacy Data Transfer (LDT) date was 31.05.2025.

I am facing an issue where the P&L accounts in the 2026 Trial Balance are showing cumulative balances (not cleared). To resolve this, I am trying to finalize the settings in the "Make Company Code Settings - Asset Accounting Specific" app, but I want to ensure I enter the correct values to avoid any further inconsistencies.

Could you please clarify the following for our scenario?

  1. Legacy Data Transfer Segment: Should I change the "Transfer Status" from "Ongoing" to "Completed" at this stage to allow a clean balance carryforward?
  2. Highest Fiscal Year: Since we are now in 2026, should this field be set to 2025 or 2026 to close the previous year and allow 2026 postings?
  3. Closed Fiscal Year: Under the Ledger settings, should the "Closed Fiscal Year" be set to 2024 (the year prior to our LDT)?

Our main goal is to successfully run the Balance Carryforward (FAGLGV) so that the 2026 opening balances are correct and P&L accounts are zeroed out.

Any guidance on the mandatory sequence for these settings would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks in advance!

entry.title: Unable to Delete Parked Document for Supplier Down Payment – Posting Period Error in Workflow
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

We are facing an issue related to the Supplier Down Payment workflow and would appreciate your guidance.

Scenario:

  • A user posted a Supplier Down Payment Request (Document Type: KA) through the “Verify Supplier Down Payment” app in February.

  • Both approvals were completed in March.

  • Since the original posting date is in February and the posting period for February is now closed, the workflow fails during the background posting step with a posting period error.

Error:
Posting period 002 2026 not open.

Troubleshooting Done:

  • We recreated the same scenario in the QAS system.

  • Even after opening the February posting period, the issue still occurs QAS

Requirement:
The user would like to delete the parked document that is currently stuck in the workflow.

Question:
Is there any standard way to delete or cancel this parked Supplier Down Payment document when it is stuck due to posting period issues in workflow?

Any suggestions or recommended approach would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you.

Rohini

entry.title: Re: FULL OUTER JOIN in ABAP SQL?
entry.content:
Hi,

I just tried the following in our dev system with very limited data (4-digit line count in each table). The performance was .... I just did one run.

    SELECT coalesce( ekbe~ebeln, ekkn~ebeln ) as ebeln,
           coalesce( ekbe~ebelp, ekkn~ebelp ) as ebelp,
           coalesce( ekbe~zekkn, ekkn~zekkn ) as zekkn,
           ekbe~bewtp,
           ekkn~loekz
    FROM ekbe
      LEFT OUTER JOIN ekkn
        ON    ekbe~ebeln = ekkn~ebeln
          AND ekbe~ebelp = ekkn~ebelp
          AND ekbe~zekkn = ekkn~zekkn
*    WHERE ...
    UNION
    SELECT coalesce( ekbe~ebeln, ekkn~ebeln ) as ebeln,
           coalesce( ekbe~ebelp, ekkn~ebelp ) as ebelp,
           coalesce( ekbe~zekkn, ekkn~zekkn ) as zekkn,
           ekbe~bewtp,
           ekkn~loekz
    FROM ekkn
      LEFT OUTER JOIN ekbe
        ON    ekbe~ebeln = ekkn~ebeln
          AND ekbe~ebelp = ekkn~ebelp
          AND ekbe~zekkn = ekkn~zekkn
*    WHERE ...
    INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_result).

    out->write( lt_result ).

The idea is to do both "parts" of a FULL OUTER JOIN seperately and then unite them with a UNION. This one especially without the ALL addition in order to remove the duplicates which are the datasets which exists in both tables. 

Best regards
Michael

entry.title: Re: Can you remember the first time you heard of SAP
entry.content:
After spending 10 years managing various ERPs such as Epicor, QAD, and iCube, I began my SAP journey in S/4 HANA in 2021.

I discovered several similarities with the previous ERPs, but to be honest, as I continue to learn more each day, I am very proud of my decision to pursue a career in SAP.

This is not just an ERP system like the ones I dealt with before; it's a complete ecosystem. Now, I oversee all integrations with S/4 HANA, including Spend Management (Ariba, Concur) and HR (SuccessFactors), utilizing BTP and CPI.

I'm also exploring the fantastic tools available for integration and automation.

Chafik

entry.title: Re: Create an instance of SAP Data Privacy Integration (N...
entry.content:
Turns out it is part RTFM but part the FM is written poorly

You've subscribed to SAP Data Privacy Integration NextGen in the subaccount on SAP Business Technology Platform in which you've subscribed to SAP Digital Manufacturing.


SAP came back in a case suggesting, that I shall use Subaccount --> Services --> Subscribe, rather than creating a service instance by hand.

This worked insofar, that I now can access the application. So I will mark this as answered.

Thanks / Cheers

J
